stories.headline: Thunder edge Nuggets 129-126 on Gilgeous-Alexander buzzer-beater
stories.summary:
Shai Gilgeous-Alexander buried a step-back three-pointer over Spencer Jones with 2.7 seconds remaining Monday night, lifting the Oklahoma City Thunder past the Denver Nuggets 129-126 at Paycom Center. The shot capped a 35-point, nine-rebound, 15-assist performance for Gilgeous-Alexander, who also tied Wilt Chamberlain's NBA record with his 126th consecutive 20-point game.

Nikola Jokic posted a triple-double of 32 points, 14 rebounds and 13 assists for Denver, while Aaron Gordon scored 23 points in his second game back from a hamstring strain. Tim Hardaway Jr. added 28 points on 8-of-12 shooting from three-point range off the bench.

The loss dropped the Nuggets to 39-26 and sixth place in the Western Conference standings. It was Denver's third consecutive loss to Oklahoma City, the team that eliminated them in last season's playoffs.

Gordon, whose minutes remain restricted by the coaching staff, said after the game he feels ready to return to a full workload but acknowledged the decision rests with coaches and trainers. His availability for back-to-back games remains uncertain.

stories.headline: Broncos release Dre Greenlaw, re-sign Alex Singleton as NFL free agency opens
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos released inside linebacker Dre Greenlaw on Monday as the NFL's legal tampering period opened, cutting ties with the marquee signing of their 2025 free-agent class after just one injury-plagued season. Greenlaw played only 10 games in 2025 and finished with 43 tackles; his release saves the team approximately $6 million in cap space.

Also Monday, Denver re-signed veteran ILB Alex Singleton to a two-year deal worth $15.5 million total. Singleton led the team with 135 tackles in 2025 and is described as a valued piece of coordinator Vance Joseph's defense despite limitations in pass coverage.

The Broncos also agreed to re-sign running back J.K. Dobbins during the tampering window, continuing a pattern of retaining their own players while rivals in the AFC West made aggressive outside moves. The Las Vegas Raiders signed linebackers Nakobe Dean and Quay Walker, while the Kansas City Chiefs landed Super Bowl MVP Kenneth Walker III.

Denver has yet to add a notable outside free agent as of Monday afternoon, raising questions about whether the front office will address remaining roster needs — particularly at inside linebacker and safety — before or during April's NFL Draft.

stories.headline: Kyle Karros homers twice as Rockies top Athletics 11-6 in spring training
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies infield prospect Kyle Karros hit two home runs Monday as the Rockies defeated the Oakland Athletics 11-6 in a spring training game in Arizona. The performance highlighted a productive day for the young Rockies roster as Colorado works through its 16th spring training contest.

The Rockies enter the exhibition schedule with continued focus on developing younger players after finishing 29th in MLB in reliever ERA last season. New bullpen coach Matt Buschmann has emphasized workload management with a young pitching staff as a priority heading into 2026.

Manager Warren Schaeffer has stressed open communication as a core value of the rebuilding club's culture, with the coaching staff working to model that approach for players. The Rockies hold a 8-7 spring record with two consecutive wins entering the week.

stories.headline: Colorado House passes union-organizing bill headed for likely Polis veto
stories.summary:
The Colorado House passed House Bill 1005 on a 42-22 party-line vote Monday, advancing legislation that would repeal a state law requiring newly formed unions to win a second election — with a threshold as high as 75% approval — before negotiating union dues provisions with employers. Every House Democrat supported the measure; every Republican opposed it.

The bill now moves to the Senate, where it is expected to pass, and then to Gov. Jared Polis, who has already signaled he will veto it as he did an identical measure last year. Polis spokeswoman Shelby Wieman said Monday his position has not changed, and no new negotiations between business groups and unions are currently underway.

Sponsors Rep. Javier Mabrey and Rep. Jennifer Bacon, both Denver Democrats, argued that rising costs and erosion of federal worker protections under the Trump administration make the bill more urgent than ever. Union supporters have increasingly shifted focus toward securing commitments from Polis' likely Democratic successor rather than changing the current governor's mind.

The legislature is also advancing several related Democratic priority bills this week, including measures to restrict certain business tax incentives and redirect the savings to a tax credit for lower-income families with children.

stories.headline: Denver City Council committee weighs sentencing reform for municipal court cases
stories.summary:
A Denver City Council committee is considering changes to sentencing guidelines for cases tried in municipal court, a move that sponsors say would reduce penalties that disproportionately burden low-income and working-class residents. The proposal represents a potentially significant shift in how Denver handles lower-level offenses at the municipal level.

The effort could put the council on a collision course with Mayor Mike Johnston, whose support for the measure has not been secured. Details of the specific sentencing changes under consideration were not immediately released by council members.

The proposal is part of a broader national conversation about criminal justice reform playing out in cities across the country. Denver's municipal court handles a wide range of cases including traffic violations, low-level drug offenses, and other misdemeanor-level city code violations.

stories.headline: Colorado faces $306 million TABOR refund it may not legally owe, complicating budget fix
stories.summary:
Colorado is set to issue $306 million in taxpayer refunds this year under the Taxpayer's Bill of Rights — money that state analysts say may not actually be owed due to a quirk created by last year's federal tax cuts. The issue stems from the federal One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law July 4, which cut federal income taxes and in turn reduced Colorado's state tax collections because the state's tax code is linked to the federal code.

Because the law was signed four days into the current fiscal year, the revenue reduction does not appear on the books for the prior fiscal year under standard accounting rules — potentially triggering a $306 million TABOR refund obligation that would not exist if the timing had been slightly different. Gov. Jared Polis has asked the legislature to recover the money by reducing future TABOR refunds, but legislative attorneys are not convinced the fix is legally sound.

The problem compounds the state's existing $1 billion budget gap. Most of the refund money — $183 million — would go to local governments to cover the homestead property tax exemption for seniors and disabled veterans, with the remainder issued as sales tax refunds to individual filers. Taxpayers earning the median income of around $95,000 would receive roughly $25 each.

Joint Budget Committee Staff Director Craig Harper acknowledged the logic of Polis' request but said legal and practical challenges loom large. Resolving the question could determine whether lawmakers have additional resources to avoid deeper cuts to public services.

stories.headline: Colorado proposes $50 million cut to medical residency funding; hospitals push back
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis' administration is proposing to cut approximately $50 million from a program that helps teaching hospitals cover the indirect costs of training medical residents — a move the state is considering as part of its effort to close an $850 million budget hole. The Joint Budget Committee could vote on the proposal as early as Monday.

Hospitals and medical educators argue the cut is shortsighted, warning it would weaken a physician pipeline that Colorado depends on, particularly in rural and underserved areas. Doctors who complete residencies in the state frequently remain to practice here, and advocates say cutting training support would accelerate existing physician shortages on the Western Slope and elsewhere.

Because the program draws federal Medicaid matching funds, the actual general fund savings would be only $14.6 million of the $50.6 million reduction — with the remainder representing federal dollars Colorado would forfeit by pulling back state contributions. The state Department of Health Care Policy and Financing, which administers Medicaid, has backed the cuts as a necessary budget measure.

The proposal illustrates the difficult trade-offs facing lawmakers as they trim Medicaid spending, where nearly every dollar of state savings eliminates at least an additional dollar of federal funding. Opponents contend the long-term cost to the health care workforce will far exceed the short-term budget relief.

stories.headline: Regis University names first female president in its nearly 150-year history
stories.summary:
Regis University announced Monday that Shawna Cooper Whitehead will become the Denver-based Jesuit Catholic institution's 29th president and its first female president in its nearly 150-year history. She will begin the role July 1, succeeding interim president Rev. D. Scott Hendrickson.

Cooper Whitehead comes from Boston College, where she has served as vice president of student affairs since 2021. Her tenure there included raising more than $20 million in philanthropic funding for student affairs and training faculty, staff, and students in Indigenous-rooted restorative justice practices.

Her career in Jesuit and Catholic higher education also includes roles at Seton Hall University, Loyola University Chicago, the University of Chicago, and Northwestern University, among others. Board of Trustees chair Derek Scarth cited her record of student-centered leadership and commitment to educational equity as central to her selection.

"I just can't wait to be a part of this community," Cooper Whitehead said in a university news release. "My experience in Jesuit higher education has brought out the best in me professionally and personally, and I hope to give that same gift to all our students."

stories.headline: Critical fire weather conditions expected to expand across Denver metro by Thursday
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service in Denver/Boulder is forecasting critical fire weather conditions to develop across the Front Range and eastern plains by Thursday, with humidity dropping near 15 percent and winds exceeding 25 mph. The most dangerous conditions are expected from Fort Collins and Greeley south through Denver, Castle Rock, and Colorado Springs.

Conditions are forecast to worsen further by Saturday, when humidity could fall below 10 percent while strong gusty winds persist — a combination that can allow fires to spread rapidly. Warm, dry weather is expected to begin building Tuesday and Wednesday before the most severe conditions arrive.

Authorities are urging residents to avoid activities that could ignite fires, including outdoor burning, and to secure trailer chains and properly dispose of cigarettes as vegetation continues to dry across the region. Additional fire weather watches or warnings may be issued later this week as winds strengthen across eastern Colorado.

The fire danger forecast follows a brief period of mild, pleasant weather that has brought temperatures above 70 degrees to the Denver metro. A weak cold front Tuesday evening will provide only a brief and modest cooling before winds and dry conditions return.

stories.headline: Fort Carson soldier killed in Iran during Operation Epic Fury
stories.summary:
A Fort Carson-based soldier, Sgt. Benjamin N. Pennington, 26, was killed Sunday of injuries sustained during a U.S.-Israel military operation against Iran known as Operation Epic Fury, according to a Department of Defense news release. Pennington is among the U.S. service members killed since the operation began Feb. 28.

The Pentagon has reported a total of seven U.S. service members killed since the conflict began, with an eighth dying from health complications. The White House has said the operation is expected to last four to six weeks.

Fort Carson, located south of Colorado Springs, is one of the largest U.S. Army installations in the country and home to tens of thousands of active-duty soldiers and their families. Pennington's death marks a direct and personal impact of the conflict on the Colorado military community.

The Weld County Coroner will release additional information after next of kin are notified. No further details about the circumstances of Pennington's death were immediately available.

stories.headline: Fatal crash closes southbound I-25 for hours north of Denver
stories.summary:
A collision between a Ford Super Duty pickup truck and a work van in a road construction zone near Mead killed the pickup's driver early Monday morning, closing southbound Interstate 25 for roughly five hours. The crash occurred around 5:55 a.m. near milepost 243, approaching the Longmont/Lyons exit, according to the Colorado State Patrol.

The Colorado Department of Transportation shut down I-25 southbound at milepost 243 while investigators worked the scene. The interstate reopened shortly before 11 a.m. The driver of the work van reported no injuries.

The Weld County Coroner will identify the deceased driver after next of kin are notified. The State Patrol's vehicular crimes unit is investigating the cause of the crash.

The closure caused significant delays for morning commuters traveling south toward the Denver metro area from northern Colorado communities.

stories.headline: Colorado Parks and Wildlife Commission advances citizen petition to ban fur sales statewide
stories.summary:
The Colorado Parks and Wildlife Commission voted Monday to advance a citizen petition that would ban the sale of furs in the state, sending the measure to the formal rulemaking process despite opposition from CPW staff, agency attorneys, and the department's own director. The vote was marked by confusion, with commissioners acknowledging uncertainty about precisely what motion they were approving.

The petition was brought by Samantha Miller of the Center for Biological Diversity, who previously led the campaign behind a failed mountain lion, bobcat, and lynx hunting ban. CPW's Carnivore and Furbearer Program Manager presented staff's recommendation against the petition before the vote.

The outcome drew scrutiny over alleged communications from the governor's office. Miller, in a social media post, claimed she had been directed by the governor's office to ensure animal-rights advocates turned out in force at the Denver meeting, a claim the governor's office disputed. A spokesperson said Polis did not have a position on the petition and that CPW staff recommended opposing it.

Because CPW is a Type 1 agency, the governor's authority is limited to budget management and does not extend to rulemaking or regulatory decisions — making any political influence over the commission's vote a potential legal and ethical concern. The matter will now proceed through a rulemaking process that could include a follow-up hearing in Grand Junction.

weather.notable: About 16 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: You know what's a fun game? Trying to remember where you parked your car. It's like hide and seek, except you didn't plan to play.

topStory.headline: Oil Prices Swing Wildly as Trump Signals War With Iran May Be Near End, While Officials Send Mixed Messages
topStory.summary:
Oil prices swung dramatically over a 24-hour period as conflicting signals from the Trump administration created uncertainty about the trajectory of the ongoing U.S.-Israeli war against Iran. Brent crude surged to $119.50 per barrel Monday — a roughly 32 percent spike — before plunging to approximately $91.70 per barrel after President Donald Trump told CBS News the conflict was "very complete, pretty much." U.S. crude similarly fell around 5 percent to approximately $86 per barrel following Trump's remarks.

The president's comments stood in direct contrast to statements from his own defense secretary. Pete Hegseth told CBS News's "60 Minutes" that "this is only the beginning," while Trump was simultaneously signaling the war's conclusion elsewhere. Trump also issued a stark warning on social media, threatening to strike Iran "twenty times harder" if Tehran interfered with oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of global oil and seaborne gas tankers typically pass. The strait has been effectively closed for approximately one week.

Iran responded defiantly to the American pressure. Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, reposted Trump's threat on X and warned the U.S. government to "beware, lest you are the ones who are eliminated." Iran's Revolutionary Guards separately vowed they would not allow "one liter of oil" to leave the region if U.S. and Israeli attacks continued. Meanwhile, Iran's new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, was formally appointed, succeeding his father who was killed in U.S.-Israeli strikes more than a week ago.

Global markets, which had been rattled by the energy price surge, rebounded after Trump's comments eased fears of a prolonged conflict. European indexes recovered sharply on Tuesday, with Germany's DAX up 2.32 percent, France's CAC 40 up 1.86 percent, and Britain's FTSE 100 up approximately 1.67 percent. Asian markets also posted gains, with South Korea's Kospi rising as much as 6 percent and Japan's Nikkei 225 climbing 2.5 percent.

The conflict's ripple effects are being felt well beyond financial markets. Vietnam, heavily dependent on Middle Eastern energy imports, reported gasoline prices up 32 percent, diesel up 56 percent, and kerosene up 80 percent since hostilities began, prompting the government to urge businesses to allow employees to work from home. Gulf states including Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates reported intercepting Iranian missiles and drones targeting critical infrastructure. European Union Council President António Costa warned that Russia was emerging as the sole beneficiary of the conflict, gaining revenue from higher energy prices and reduced Western focus on Ukraine, as French President Emmanuel Macron indicated European nations may deploy naval vessels to escort commercial shipping once the most intense phase of fighting subsides.
